steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank must complete a logical inference from one fact: all Mixe-Zoquean loanwords in Mayan languages come specifically from the Zoque branch, not from the Mixe branch. The correct choice must explain why the borrowings are Zoque-only, using only information the passage provides.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the language of the Olmec civilization contributed words not only to Mayan languages but also to other languages in the Mixe-Zoquean family.: The passage discusses only Mayan loanwords; it says nothing about borrowing into other Mixe-Zoquean languages. This introduces a claim the passage cannot support. ✗
  • Choice B — North Central Mixe and the other languages of the Mixe branch likely supplanted the languages of the Zoque branch sometime before 1500 BCE.: The passage says Zoque languages are still spoken today as present-day representatives, so they were not supplanted. This also inverts the implication: if Mixe had replaced Zoque, we would expect Mixe words in Mayan, not Zoque words. ✗
  • Choice C — the Mixe-Zoquean family had already diverged into the Mixe and Zoque branches by the time the Olmecs became the prevailing power in the region.: If the family were still unified during Olmec dominance, borrowings could come from anywhere in the family. The fact that all loanwords are Zoque-only means borrowing happened after the split, when Zoque was already a distinct branch. This is the inference the clue “all those words derive from the Zoque branch” directly supports. ✓
  • Choice D — the language of the Olmec civilization was likely the founding language of the family that includes Mayan languages.: Mayan languages are described as “otherwise unrelated to the Mixe-Zoquean family” and borrowed words from it; they are not derived from it. This misreads the relationship between the two families. ✗
That is the correct answer.
